Page 1

Bearing 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
September-October, 1991

[Logo: Cascade Orienteering Club, Seattle U.S.A.]

WASHINGTON STATE CHAMPIONSHIPS

Two Day B-Meet at Moran State Park, September 7 and 8
by Rick Hood and Peter Golde (lonely meet crew)

The 1991 Washington State Championships will be held this year at Moran State Park on Orcas Island. Rick Hood will be the Meet Director and Peter Golde will be the Course Designer. Peter has designed four courses per day (blue, red/green, orange/yellow, and white). If you are wondering what Peter's courses are like just ask someone who ran at Drunken Charlie this July; the general consensus was that Peter's courses were clean, challenging and fun.

Although we are well on the way to having a great meet, what we need now to guarantee the meet a success and enjoyable is some more members willing to help out at the meet itself. Everybody who helps will be able to run both days if they want. We'd really like to have 6-8 people per day who would be willing to help for 1.5 to 2 hours. Please call Rick at 367-2606 if you are willing. Both Rick and Peter really appreciate it!

The Event Center will be at Mountain Lake Campground (Look for Rick's white van and the COC banner). Registration will be $6 for one day, and $10 for two days.. Maps will be two half maps.

On Saturday everyone must copy their own map during the 5min pre-start period. On Sunday, those who opted for the $10 package, will get a pre-marked map. Further, Sunday will feature a modified chase start. (Saturday's results and Sunday's start times will be posted at the Event Center and Camp Orkila Saturday evening.) Purple championship, and 1st, 2nd, 3rd place ribbons will be awarded for each class. (Classes with less than 5 orienteers will be combined.) The awards will be handed out at Sunday's finish around 1pm. If you want to stay or eat at Camp Orkila, call Dave Petty at 822-4223 ASAP.

Remember this is the first of the Pig War III Challenge meets (Mt. Douglas is the second meet). We need a big turnout to make certain that we get the Pig War cup back.

Lastly, look for info regarding the MushBall Challenge I.

Additional information and ferry schedules are shown on the flyer included with this issue.

[Map Image: Site map - Ft Casey, Moran]

COMING MEETS:

FORT CASEY - Saturday, October 19

Fort Casey is an abandoned fort on the west side of Whidbey Island, about 25 miles north of Clinton on the Mukilteo ferry run. Fort Casey is always a popular meet location, and we expect a good crowd when we return to Fort Casey this October. This will be a standard Club meet, with a picnic afterwards.

HAMLIN PARK NIGHT-O - Saturday, Nov 2

If you've never been to a Night-O, this is your chance. Special, reflecting markers are used. Be prepared that there may be some "spooks" left over from Halloween that will make this a very exciting meet. Hamlin Park is about 160th on 15th NE (west of Lake Forest Park).

Page 2

US CHAMPIONSHIPS

The US Championships will be held October 12-13 (Columbus Day weekend) near Potosi, Missouri, about 100 miles southwest of St Louis. Registration deadline to avoid a late fee is September 28. Meet Fees are $14/day ($8 juniors), T-shirts are $7.50. Camping is available in cabins at a Boy Scout camp adjacent to the meet site at $5/night, and at several campgrounds. There are motels about 20 miles away. Entry forms are available in O/NA (August) and will be available at the Moran meet.

The US Championships are a highlight of the orienteering year, and we have usually had a good representation. With the possibility of low air fares (see box below), there should be a good attendance of COC members at the championships. Check with Bruce McAlister if you would like to coordinate housing and/or transportation.

Air Fare Special:
As of August 30, your travel agent could still register you in Northwest Airlines World Perks program and qualify for two coupons for two round-trips to St Louis at $198 each. Northwest flies to St Louis via Minneapolis. Seats at this price are limited, It takes about 10 days to get the coupons, and tickets must be purchased by Sept 15, so hurry.

O' NOTES:

by Ardis, Bruce and Harvey

Northwest orienteers continue to travel. Seen at the Colorado Hi-O were Katie and Doug Sprugel, Brian Hart, Bruce McAlister and Fred Veler. Eric Bone missed some orienteering this summer while he was on a school field trip to Ecuador and the Galapagos Islands.

Returning to the Northwest are Pat Albright in September and Arne Skog is back fighting fires in Bellevue.

Len Englund, the founder of Cascade, is recovering from a bout of cancer surgery. We all wish Len a quick recovery. Other recoveries include Knut Olsen, whose knee is now back in shape, and Knut is planning on the nationals next month.

Nathan Tharp has been doing mapping for Sammamish and Nisqually this summer, and has mapped or remapped Forest Park, Big Finn Hill, Ft Steilacoom and Priest Point Park.

No weddings or births this month, but two adoptions in the Freeman/York household: Munchkit and Loki (siamese cats).

The Pacific Science Center has an orienteering related display from November - January. More in the next issue.

POTLUCK AND GENERAL MEETING

Friday, September 20

Potluck time is back! Set aside Friday, September 20 at 6:30 at Rick and Linda Hood's - 19708 15th NW. This is just east of I-5 just south of the county line. To reach Rick and Linda's, go north on 15th NW from 145th, or south from Ballinger Way. Turn at the Forest Creek Apartments on the east side of 15th. Follow "O" signs to the party room. Bring a suit for the hot-tub. Coordinate food with Linda at 367-2606.

There will be a short general meeting at the potluck to consider ongoing business, and review "how-they-ran" at Moran and Aspen Grove and the Rogaine

Board Briefs

July 11, 1991: The Board met at Dave Petty's. B. McAlister, P. Golde, A Dull, D. Atkinson, D. Petty, M. Schuh, D. Newell, H. Friedman, and R. Hood were present. Reports on past meets were made, and plans for Drunken Charlie, Woodland Park, Rogaine and Moran were reviewed. A mapping program was approved for Washington Park (Anacortes), Camp Hamilton, Evergreen H.S., and revision of the St. Edwards and Lake Tradition maps.
WSOA - The Board voted to support allocation of excess funds over $500 to member clubs.
Voted that General meetings should combine social and business aspects and hold meetings in more social settings.

August 8, 1991: The Board met at Rick and Linda Hood's. Cascade has received a request to move the 1992 USOF Convention one week ahead. The Board agreed to the move.
WSOA - There was discussion of Cascade's relation to WSOA. The Board agreed to end all present COC assignments to the WSOA board as of September. New WSOA appointments will be made at the Board meeting in September.
Schedules - Board agreed to revised fall/winter schedule (see box on page 1)
Next Meeting: September 11 at Doug and Katie Sprugels All members are always welcome at all Board meetings.

WSOA NOTES

by Bruce McAlister

Cascade members proposed at the last WSOA meeting that by-law changes be submitted to the member clubs that would, in effect, remove major map production and sponsorship A meets as primary functions of WSOA. The proposal was defeated, and A meets and new maps remain primary goals of WSOA. I think the last thing northwest orienteering needs is one more organization collecting money, holding meetings and forming committees, making maps and holding A meets. What we need is more cooperation, fewer meetings, and stronger local clubs, but that remains a minority viewpoint.

Page 3

[Graphic: Cascade Orienteering Logo]

Cascade Orienteering presents the

1991 Washington State Orienteering Championships

A Two Day B-meet at Moran St. Park

September 7 and 8.

Location: Moran State Park, Orcas Island. Day 1 registration will be at Mountain Lake Landing, with start about 1 km walk. Day 2 registration and start will be at the Little Summit on Mt. Constitution. Parking is limited to 10 cars at the Little Summit, so competitors should park at Mountain Lake Landing and carpool/shuttle from there.

Map and Courses: Moran State Park, 1989. IOF 1:15000, 5m contours. 4 courses, novice to expert. Courses are open to all competitors; novices welcome! All Washington State residents are eligible for the State Championships. Washington and B.C. residents will be scored for Pig War III, which will conclude on October 5, 6 at Mt. Douglas.

Date and Time: Saturday, September 7 and Sunday, September 8. Day 1 registration: 11:00-1:00, starts 12:00-2:00. Day 2 registration: 8:30-10:30, starts 9:00-11:00.

Cost: $6 per day.

Contacts: Rick Hood, Meet Director: (206) 367-2606. Peter Golde, Course Designer: 883-3732. Dave Petty, Orkila arrangements: 822-4223.

Accommodations: Moran State park has camping sites available: first come, first serve; call Rick for more info. YMCA Camp Orkila has 8-person cabins available at $25, and serves meals as well. Call Dave to make reservations early; no later than 8/31. Cottages, inexpensive "dorm-style" lodging, and sauna/hotsprings available at Doe Bay near the park: 376-2291.

Directions: Take Route 20 west from I-5 to San Juan ferry terminal at Anacortes. Take ferry to Orcas Island, then follow road signs to Moran State Park (15-20 minute drive from ferry). Ferry leaves Anacortes: Fri: 3:40pm, 6:10pm, 8:40pm, Sat: 12:20am, 5:25am, 6:05am, 8:30am, 10:45am. Ferry ride takes 1h 25m; those taking 10:45am ferry should be prepared to register and run immediately upon arrival at Moran. Return times, fares, Victoria sailings, and other ferry information available by calling (206) 464-2000, then pressing 5500 (touch-tone only). We encourage carpooling.

[Map Image]

Page 4

MEET RESULTS

Magnuson Park
May 22
Meet Director: Katie Sprugel/Denise Klein
Course Design: Eileen Breseman

The last of our spring Wednesday evening meets,was cold and rainy, but saw a good group, including two Girls Scout Troops - #1471 and #1730, who had been practicing orienteering, and had their first experience here on a real orienteering course.
Eileen Breseman designed the courses. Course 4 was a "Windows" course - instead of a regular map, each participant received a map which consisted of small circles or "windows" around each control location. This was the O-map for the course.
Those who braved the rain and the cold enjoyed a picnic afterwards

Drunken Charlie Lake
July 13

The promised rains never came, and the cool, cloudy weather meant another fine day at Drunken Charlie. Peter set the courses in the area north and east of Drunken Charlie Lake. Carl Coger's O-Van brightened the start area. Dave Enger came in just three minutes ahead of Victoria's Martin Pardoe on Course 5. Thanks to all who helped; your newsletter editor couldn't find the list.

As usual, we’ll start with positive roster changes as of 28 August 1991. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Margie, Eric, & Nicholas Bone, Bonnie Brannon, Bettye Christian, Becca Crofton & Wayne McDonald, Ann Dacy, Mickey Fowler, John Giaever, Mary & Michael Kenny, Pat Kennedy, Norm & Gerri King, Israel Kloss, Amanda Kolehmainen, Pete Kynion, Eric Lawson, John Ledington, Bob & Suzy Lynch, Brad Miner, Carl & Linda Moore, Debbie Newell, Dot Newell, Brian O’Flynn, Elizabeth Prindle, Jessica Rykken, Joseph Schneider, Cabe Shepard, Mark Sinclair, Gail Smith & Craig Wyss, David Soper & Denise Klein, Bob Sunderland, Walt & Nancy Turley, Sid & Janet Whaley, Jerry Wilson, Mike Wilson, Bill Yost family.

If you want to get your handsome membership card, introduce yourself to the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet.. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs’ functions. If you move, don’t forget to turn in a change of address card; you don’t want your newsletter to be late (or lost).

IOF ADOPTS NEW CONTROL DESCRIPTION SYMBOLS

Several years ago the International Orienteering Federation adopted a graphic language to describe control locations. These symbols make it possible for an orienteer to understand the control descriptions regardless of the language he speaks. These are the symbols we use on the advanced and intermediate courses at most of our meets.

Recently the IOF adopted the following ne international symbols for course decriptions:

MOVE TO COLUMN G: [Arrow Pointing into Box]

END - Moves from Column E to G: [Checkmark]

BETWEEN - Moves from Column C to G: [Symbol with two dashes]

In Column D (the control feature)
__PH_5__ __PH_0__ PILE OF STUMPS, BRANCHES OR DEBRIS: [Symbol with crossed lines/stars]
__PH_7__ __PH_2__ NARROW PASSAGE: [Symbol with two brackets facing inward]

In Column G (location of the marker)
* RAZED OR WRECKED OBJECT (NOT YET A RUIN): [Symbol with a crooked arrow]

You can pick up a complete explanation of all IOF symbols at any Cascade Meet. All you have to do is ask.

[Cartoon: __PH_0__]
Panel 1: "GENTLEMEN, FOR THE NEXT SIX WEEKS WE'LL BE PATROLLING THE SOUTHWEST TERRITORY." Panel 2: "CATFISH, YOU HAVE THE MAPS, OF COURSE?"
Panel 3: "AH'M A SEASONED SCOUT, MAJOR! AH DON'T NEED NO MAPS!" Panel 4: "THAT MAY BE TRUE, CATFISH. BUT LET'S PLAY IT SAFE. GO BACK TO YOUR BARRACKS AND GET THE MAPS." "IF YA INSIST, MAJOR."
Panel 5: "WE'LL CONTINUE OUR BRIEFING WHILE WE'RE WAITING, MEN." Panel 6: " 'SCUSE ME, MAJOR..."
Panel 7:* "...HOW DO AH TURN RIGHT OR LEFT TO GIT BACK TA M'BARRACKS?"

The Dancing Lady Rogaine

It was a cloudless sky, but the temperature in the 30s held more than a hint of fall when 26 teams set out on the Dancing Lady Rogaine at White Pass, Saturday, August 24. This was COC’s first Rogaine (the 1989 Rogaine was a WSOA event). Forest Service requirements to use the wilderness area posed constraints which limited the course design, and there were several less than optimally placed controls. Still, the area was spectacularly beautiful, offered great orienteering terrain, and minimal mosquitoes. All competitors finished with only three overtimes (although Mac and Karen MacDonald picked their own finish at Dog Lake), there were no injuries, and no lost teams. Glitches notwithstanding, everyone appeared to have a good run, with Alan Philip (VO) and Jim Traynor (CVOC) collecting all the controls in a little over seven hours (of the 8-hour Rogaine).

The Cascade Board will be considering whether to schedule Rogaines as regular features, or whether COC should schedule Rogaines at all. Preparing and running a Rogaine takes more time and effort than two or three regular orienteering events, and COC has no surplus of either volunteers or money. If a Rogaine is scheduled, what should be the time limit(s)? Should the site be a wilderness area or should an east slope area with logging, roads, etc. be used. Let your Board know your feelings.

COMING “A” MEETS AND SPECIAL EVENTS

September 28/29: Troll Cup, Upton, MA October 19/20: Heartland Championships, Kellings Lake S.P., Campbellsport, WI
Nov 2/3: US Long-O and 2-day Individual, Letchworth S.P, New York November 23/24: Western States Championships, Sunol Regional Park, Fremont, CA
* December 29: Bob Slog, St. Edwards State Park, Bothell

COC continues to rely upon a small and dedicated group for meet directors and course designers. With a club membership of about 150 and only about 15 events per year, one should only have to be a meet director once every 10 years or so. So, sign up now and rest for 9 years. We have how-to-do-it manuals, and people to help, so let’s get a few more volunteers to help out.
Denise Klein (322-7797) is our Volunteer Coordinator, and will be happy to take your name and line you up with a job in need of a volunteer.

For the latest meet information, call the ORIENTEERING HOTLINE at 783-3866

block_index.text: Bearing 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club September-October, 1991
Two Day B-Meet at Moran State Park, September 7 and 8 by Rick Hood and Peter Golde (lonely meet crew)

The 1991 Washington State Championships will be held this year at Moran State Park on Orcas Island. Rick Hood will be the Meet Director and Peter Golde will be the Course Designer. Peter has designed four courses per day (blue, red/green, orange/yellow, and white). If you are wondering what Peter's courses are like just ask someone who ran at Drunken Charlie this July; the general consensus was that Peter's courses were clean, challenging and fun.

Although we are well on the way to having a great meet, what we need now to guarantee the meet a success and enjoyable is some more members willing to help out at the meet itself. Everybody who helps will be able to run both days if they want. We'd really like to have 6-8 people per day who would be willing to help for 1.5 to 2 hours. Please call Rick at 367-2606 if you are willing. Both Rick and Peter really appreciate it!
The Event Center will be at Mountain Lake Campground (Look for Rick's white van and the COC banner). Registration will be $6 for one day, and $10 for two days.. Maps will be two half maps.

On Saturday everyone must copy their own map during the 5min pre-start period. On Sunday, those who opted for the $10 package, will get a pre-marked map. Further, Sunday will feature a modified chase start. (Saturday's results and Sunday's start times will be posted at the Event Center and Camp Orkila Saturday evening.) Purple championship, and 1st, 2nd, 3rd place ribbons will be awarded for each class. (Classes with less than 5 orienteers will be combined.) The awards will be handed out at Sunday's finish around 1pm. If you want to stay or eat at Camp Orkila, call Dave Petty at 822-4223 ASAP.
Remember this is the first of the Pig War III Challenge meets (Mt. Douglas is the second meet). We need a big turnout to make certain that we get the Pig War cup back.
Lastly, look for info regarding the MushBall Challenge I.
Additional information and ferry schedules are shown on the flyer included with this issue.

Fort Casey is an abandoned fort on the west side of Whidbey Island, about 25 miles north of Clinton on the Mukilteo ferry run. Fort Casey is always a popular meet location, and we expect a good crowd when we return to Fort Casey this October. This will be a standard Club meet, with a picnic afterwards.

If you've never been to a Night-O, this is your chance. Special, reflecting markers are used. Be prepared that there may be some "spooks" left over from Halloween that will make this a very exciting meet. Hamlin Park is about 160th on 15th NE (west of Lake Forest Park).

The US Championships will be held October 12-13 (Columbus Day weekend) near Potosi, Missouri, about 100 miles southwest of St Louis. Registration deadline to avoid a late fee is September 28. Meet Fees are $14/day ($8 juniors), T-shirts are $7.50. Camping is available in cabins at a Boy Scout camp adjacent to the meet site at $5/night, and at several campgrounds. There are motels about 20 miles away. Entry forms are available in O/NA (August) and will be available at the Moran meet.

The US Championships are a highlight of the orienteering year, and we have usually had a good representation. With the possibility of low air fares (see box below), there should be a good attendance of COC members at the championships. Check with Bruce McAlister if you would like to coordinate housing and/or transportation.

Air Fare Special: As of August 30, your travel agent could still register you in Northwest Airlines World Perks program and qualify for two coupons for two round-trips to St Louis at $198 each. Northwest flies to St Louis via Minneapolis. Seats at this price are limited, It takes about 10 days to get the coupons, and tickets must be purchased by Sept 15, so hurry.

Northwest orienteers continue to travel. Seen at the Colorado Hi-O were Katie and Doug Sprugel, Brian Hart, Bruce McAlister and Fred Veler. Eric Bone missed some orienteering this summer while he was on a school field trip to Ecuador and the Galapagos Islands.
Returning to the Northwest are Pat Albright in September and Arne Skog is back fighting fires in Bellevue.

Len Englund, the founder of Cascade, is recovering from a bout of cancer surgery. We all wish Len a quick recovery. Other recoveries include Knut Olsen, whose knee is now back in shape, and Knut is planning on the nationals next month.
Nathan Tharp has been doing mapping for Sammamish and Nisqually this summer, and has mapped or remapped Forest Park, Big Finn Hill, Ft Steilacoom and Priest Point Park.
No weddings or births this month, but two adoptions in the Freeman/York household: Munchkit and Loki (siamese cats).
The Pacific Science Center has an orienteering related display from November - January. More in the next issue.

Potluck time is back! Set aside Friday, September 20 at 6:30 at Rick and Linda Hood's - 19708 15th NW. This is just east of I-5 just south of the county line. To reach Rick and Linda's, go north on 15th NW from 145th, or south from Ballinger Way. Turn at the Forest Creek Apartments on the east side of 15th. Follow "O" signs to the party room. Bring a suit for the hot-tub. Coordinate food with Linda at 367-2606.
There will be a short general meeting at the potluck to consider ongoing business, and review "how-they-ran" at Moran and Aspen Grove and the Rogaine

July 11, 1991: The Board met at Dave Petty's. B. McAlister, P. Golde, A Dull, D. Atkinson, D. Petty, M. Schuh, D. Newell, H. Friedman, and R. Hood were present. Reports on past meets were made, and plans for Drunken Charlie, Woodland Park, Rogaine and Moran were reviewed. A mapping program was approved for Washington Park (Anacortes), Camp Hamilton, Evergreen H.S., and revision of the St. Edwards and Lake Tradition maps. WSOA - The Board voted to support allocation of excess funds over $500 to member clubs. Voted that General meetings should combine social and business aspects and hold meetings in more social settings.

August 8, 1991: The Board met at Rick and Linda Hood's. Cascade has received a request to move the 1992 USOF Convention one week ahead. The Board agreed to the move. WSOA - There was discussion of Cascade's relation to WSOA. The Board agreed to end all present COC assignments to the WSOA board as of September. New WSOA appointments will be made at the Board meeting in September. Schedules - Board agreed to revised fall/winter schedule (see box on page 1) Next Meeting: September 11 at Doug and Katie Sprugels All members are always welcome at all Board meetings.

Cascade members proposed at the last WSOA meeting that by-law changes be submitted to the member clubs that would, in effect, remove major map production and sponsorship A meets as primary functions of WSOA. The proposal was defeated, and A meets and new maps remain primary goals of WSOA. I think the last thing northwest orienteering needs is one more organization collecting money, holding meetings and forming committees, making maps and holding A meets. What we need is more cooperation, fewer meetings, and stronger local clubs, but that remains a minority viewpoint.
A Two Day B-meet at Moran St. Park

Location: Moran State Park, Orcas Island. Day 1 registration will be at Mountain Lake Landing, with start about 1 km walk. Day 2 registration and start will be at the Little Summit on Mt. Constitution. Parking is limited to 10 cars at the Little Summit, so competitors should park at Mountain Lake Landing and carpool/shuttle from there.

Map and Courses: Moran State Park, 1989. IOF 1:15000, 5m contours. 4 courses, novice to expert. Courses are open to all competitors; novices welcome! All Washington State residents are eligible for the State Championships. Washington and B.C. residents will be scored for Pig War III, which will conclude on October 5, 6 at Mt. Douglas.
Date and Time: Saturday, September 7 and Sunday, September 8. Day 1 registration: 11:00-1:00, starts 12:00-2:00. Day 2 registration: 8:30-10:30, starts 9:00-11:00.
Contacts: Rick Hood, Meet Director: (206) 367-2606. Peter Golde, Course Designer: 883-3732. Dave Petty, Orkila arrangements: 822-4223.

Accommodations: Moran State park has camping sites available: first come, first serve; call Rick for more info. YMCA Camp Orkila has 8-person cabins available at $25, and serves meals as well. Call Dave to make reservations early; no later than 8/31. Cottages, inexpensive "dorm-style" lodging, and sauna/hotsprings available at Doe Bay near the park: 376-2291.

Directions: Take Route 20 west from I-5 to San Juan ferry terminal at Anacortes. Take ferry to Orcas Island, then follow road signs to Moran State Park (15-20 minute drive from ferry). Ferry leaves Anacortes: Fri: 3:40pm, 6:10pm, 8:40pm, Sat: 12:20am, 5:25am, 6:05am, 8:30am, 10:45am. Ferry ride takes 1h 25m; those taking 10:45am ferry should be prepared to register and run immediately upon arrival at Moran. Return times, fares, Victoria sailings, and other ferry information available by calling (206) 464-2000, then pressing 5500 (touch-tone only). We encourage carpooling.
Magnuson Park May 22 Meet Director: Katie Sprugel/Denise Klein Course Design: Eileen Breseman

The last of our spring Wednesday evening meets,was cold and rainy, but saw a good group, including two Girls Scout Troops - #1471 and #1730, who had been practicing orienteering, and had their first experience here on a real orienteering course. Eileen Breseman designed the courses. Course 4 was a "Windows" course - instead of a regular map, each participant received a map which consisted of small circles or "windows" around each control location. This was the O-map for the course. Those who braved the rain and the cold enjoyed a picnic afterwards
Drunken Charlie Lake July 13 Meet Director: Bruce McAlister Course Design: Peter Golde Timing:D. Tallent, C McNabb, et al Marker Pickup: Sharp, Reddick

The promised rains never came, and the cool, cloudy weather meant another fine day at Drunken Charlie. Peter set the courses in the area north and east of Drunken Charlie Lake. Carl Coger's O-Van brightened the start area. Dave Enger came in just three minutes ahead of Victoria's Martin Pardoe on Course 5. Thanks to all who helped; your newsletter editor couldn't find the list.

Woodland Park August 11 57 participants Meet Director: Ardis Dull Course Design: Eric/Nicholas/Margie Bone Course Setter: Bruce McAlister Registration: Harvey Friedman It was a warm and sunny Sunday morning as almost 60 orienteers returned to Woodland Park. Following the meet there was a picnic long into the afternoon.

If you want to get your handsome membership card, introduce yourself to the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet.. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs’ functions. If you move, don’t forget to turn in a change of address card; you don’t want your newsletter to be late (or lost).
IOF ADOPTS NEW CONTROL DESCRIPTION SYMBOLS

Several years ago the International Orienteering Federation adopted a graphic language to describe control locations. These symbols make it possible for an orienteer to understand the control descriptions regardless of the language he speaks. These are the symbols we use on the advanced and intermediate courses at most of our meets.
Recently the IOF adopted the following ne international symbols for course decriptions:
MOVE TO COLUMN G: [Arrow Pointing into Box]
END - Moves from Column E to G: [Checkmark]
BETWEEN - Moves from Column C to G: [Symbol with two dashes]

In Column D (the control feature) HEDGE: [Symbol with three circles connected by lines] PILE OF STUMPS, BRANCHES OR DEBRIS: [Symbol with crossed lines/stars] POWER POLE: [Symbol of a circle with a cross] NARROW PASSAGE: [Symbol with two brackets facing inward]
In Column G (location of the marker) RAZED OR WRECKED OBJECT (NOT YET A RUIN): [Symbol with a crooked arrow]
You can pick up a complete explanation of all IOF symbols at any Cascade Meet. All you have to do is ask.

[Cartoon: CATFISH by Bollen & Peterman] Panel 1: "GENTLEMEN, FOR THE NEXT SIX WEEKS WE'LL BE PATROLLING THE SOUTHWEST TERRITORY." Panel 2: "CATFISH, YOU HAVE THE MAPS, OF COURSE?" Panel 3: "AH'M A SEASONED SCOUT, MAJOR! AH DON'T NEED NO MAPS!" Panel 4: "THAT MAY BE TRUE, CATFISH. BUT LET'S PLAY IT SAFE. GO BACK TO YOUR BARRACKS AND GET THE MAPS." "IF YA INSIST, MAJOR." Panel 5: "WE'LL CONTINUE OUR BRIEFING WHILE WE'RE WAITING, MEN." Panel 6: " 'SCUSE ME, MAJOR..." Panel 7: "...HOW DO AH TURN RIGHT OR LEFT TO GIT BACK TA M'BARRACKS?"

It was a cloudless sky, but the temperature in the 30s held more than a hint of fall when 26 teams set out on the Dancing Lady Rogaine at White Pass, Saturday, August 24. This was COC’s first Rogaine (the 1989 Rogaine was a WSOA event). Forest Service requirements to use the wilderness area posed constraints which limited the course design, and there were several less than optimally placed controls. Still, the area was spectacularly beautiful, offered great orienteering terrain, and minimal mosquitoes. All competitors finished with only three overtimes (although Mac and Karen MacDonald picked their own finish at Dog Lake), there were no injuries, and no lost teams. Glitches notwithstanding, everyone appeared to have a good run, with Alan Philip (VO) and Jim Traynor (CVOC) collecting all the controls in a little over seven hours (of the 8-hour Rogaine).

The Cascade Board will be considering whether to schedule Rogaines as regular features, or whether COC should schedule Rogaines at all. Preparing and running a Rogaine takes more time and effort than two or three regular orienteering events, and COC has no surplus of either volunteers or money. If a Rogaine is scheduled, what should be the time limit(s)? Should the site be a wilderness area or should an east slope area with logging, roads, etc. be used. Let your Board know your feelings.
Dancing Lady Rogaine August 24 8-hr, 4-hr 19 controls, 1000 pts maximum

COC continues to rely upon a small and dedicated group for meet directors and course designers. With a club membership of about 150 and only about 15 events per year, one should only have to be a meet director once every 10 years or so. So, sign up now and rest for 9 years. We have how-to-do-it manuals, and people to help, so let’s get a few more volunteers to help out. Denise Klein (322-7797) is our Volunteer Coordinator, and will be happy to take your name and line you up with a job in need of a volunteer.
For the latest meet information, call the ORIENTEERING HOTLINE at 783-3866
